items.q: What is lag time on a storm hydrograph?
items.correct: The time between peak rainfall and peak river discharge — affected by drainage basin shape, land use, soil saturation and vegetation.
items.misconception: Lag time is how long the storm itself lasts.
items.random_wrong: Lag time is the delay between thunder and lightning.
items.why: Students misread the graph. Lag time is the gap between maximum rain and maximum river flow — it tells you how quickly the basin responds, not the duration of rainfall.

items.correct: The combined loss of water from a surface through evaporation (from soil and water bodies) and transpiration (from plant leaves).
items.misconception: It is only water evaporating from puddles and lakes.
items.random_wrong: It is the process of rain falling from clouds.
items.why: Students focus only on evaporation. Transpiration from forests can move enormous quantities of water — a key concept for understanding deforestation's effect on the water cycle.

items.q: How do humans affect the carbon cycle?
items.correct: By burning fossil fuels (releasing stored geological carbon to the atmosphere) and deforestation (reducing the biosphere store and releasing carbon).
items.misconception: Humans only affect the carbon cycle by breathing out CO2.
items.random_wrong: Humans affect the carbon cycle by eating plants.
items.why: Human respiration is balanced (we eat carbon that plants captured). The disruption is shifting carbon from long-term geological stores into the active atmospheric cycle.

items.correct: Time between peak rainfall and peak discharge
items.misconception: Time between storm start and storm end
items.why: Correct answer: Time between peak rainfall and peak discharge. “Time between storm start and storm end” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Storm Desmond (2015) brought how much rainfall in 24 hours to Honister?
items.why: It's 341 mm. “141 mm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate average annual UK precipitation in mm:
items.why: The real figure is 1150. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
